--- SOURCE 1: Bracketing (phenomenology) - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)

SUMMARY:
**Bracketing** (German: _Einklammerung_; also called **phenomenological reduction**, **transcendental reduction** or **phenomenological _epoché_**) means looking at a situation and refraining from judgement and biased opinions to wholly understand an experience.#cite_note-1) The preliminary step in the philosophical movement of phenomenology "Phenomenology (philosophy)") is to suspend judgment#cite_note-2) (_i.e._, epoché) about the natural world and instead, to focus on analysis of experience. Bracketing (or _epoché_) is a preliminary act in the phenomenological analysis, conceived by Husserl as the suspension of the trust in the objectivity of the world.#cite_note-7)#cite_note-8) It involves setting aside the question of the real existence of a contemplated object, as well as all other questions about the object's physical or objective nature; these questions are left to the natural sciences.#cite_note-9)#cite_note-10) 8.   **^#cite_ref-8 "Jump up")**"Husserl, Edmund | Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy".

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Necessary reductive first step in phenomenology

For the explaining of complex phenomena using more fundamental phenomena, see [Reductionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reductionism "Reductionism").

**Bracketing** ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language "German language"): _Einklammerung_; also called **phenomenological reduction**, **transcendental reduction** or **phenomenological _epoché_**) means looking at a situation and refraining from judgement and biased opinions to wholly understand an experience.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-1) The preliminary step in the philosophical movement of [phenomenology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)") is to [suspend judgment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suspension_of_judgment "Suspension of judgment")[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-2) (_i.e._, [epoché](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epoch%C3%A9 "Epoché")) about the natural world and instead, to focus on analysis of experience. Suspending judgement involves stripping away every connotation and assumption made about an object. Its earliest conception can be traced back to [Immanuel Kant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") who argued that the only reality that one can know is the one each individual experiences in their mind (or Phenomena). [Edmund Husserl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl"), building on Kant’s ideas, first proposed bracketing in 1913, to help better understand another’s phenomena.

Overview
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bracketing_(phenomenology)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

### Immanuel Kant

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bracketing_(phenomenology)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Immanuel Kant")]

Though it was formally developed by [Edmund Husserl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl") (1859–1938), phenomenology can be understood as an outgrowth of the influential ideas of [Immanuel Kant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") (1724–1804). Attempting to resolve some of the key intellectual debates of his era, Kant argued that _[Noumena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noumenon "Noumenon")_ (fundamentally unknowable things-in-themselves) must be distinguished from _[Phenomena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenon "Phenomenon")_ (the world as it appears to the mind).[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-3) Kant, commonly misconceived as arguing that humans cannot have direct access to [reality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reality#Western_philosophy "Reality"), but only to the [contents of their minds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mental_content "Mental content"), argued rather that what is experienced in the mind is reality to us. Phenomenology grew out of this conception of phenomena and studies the meaning of isolated phenomena as directly connected to our minds. According to The Columbia Encyclopedia, "Modern philosophers have used 'phenomenon' to designate what is apprehended before judgment is applied."[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-Columbia-4) This may not be possible if [observation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Observation "Observation") is [theory-laden](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theory-laden "Theory-laden").

### Husserl and _Epoché_

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bracketing_(phenomenology)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Husserl and Epoché")]

Edmund Husserl included the ideas of Kant in developing his concept of bracketing, also referred to as _epoché_. Though Husserl likely began developing the method of bracketing around 1906, his book, _Ideas_, introduced it when it was published in 1913.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-:0-5) Husserl reinterpreted and revitalized the _[epoché](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epoch%C3%A9 "Epoché")_ of [Pyrrhonism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pyrrhonism "Pyrrhonism") as a permanent way of challenging the [dogmatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") naivete of life in the “natural attitude” and motivating the transformation to _theoria_, or the theoretical attitude of the disinterested spectator, which is essential both to modern science and to a genuine transcendental philosophy.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-6)

Bracketing (or _epoché_) is a preliminary act in the phenomenological [analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Analysis "Analysis"), conceived by Husserl as the suspension of the trust in the objectivity of the world.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-8) It involves setting aside the question of the real existence of a contemplated object, as well as all other questions about the object's physical or objective nature; these questions are left to the [natural sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences").[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-9)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-10)

For example, the act of seeing a horse qualifies as an experience, whether one sees the horse in person, in a dream, or in a hallucination. 'Bracketing' the horse suspends any judgement about the horse as _[noumenon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noumenon "Noumenon")_, and instead analyses the _phenomenon_ of the horse as constituted in intentional acts.

Bracketing may also be understood in terms of the [phenomenological](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)") activity. It is supposed to make possible: the "unpacking" of phenomena, or, in other words, systematically peeling away their symbolic meanings like layers of an onion until only the thing itself as meant and experienced remains. Thus, one's subjective intending of the bracketed phenomenon is examined and analyzed in phenomenological purity.

Additionally, Husserl differentiated two types of bracketing which he called universal epoché and local epoché.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-:0-5) Universal epoché requires the suspension of assumptions regarding all aspects of existence. Local epoché can be understood as the suspension of assumptions regarding a certain set of particular assumptions, presumably pertaining to whatever is being examined. Husserl viewed universal epoché as stronger than local epoché.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracketing_(phenomenology)#cite_note-:0-5) To return to the example of the horse, applying local epoché would include suspending all prior assumptions regarding that particular horse, such as its appearance or temperament. Applying universal epoché in this example would likely mean suspending all assumptions regarding all horses or even all animals or all forms of life in general.

See also
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bracketing_(phenomenology)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

# What Is Bracketing In Qualitative Research?

[Saul McLeod, PhD](https://www.simplypsychology.org/author/saulmcleod)

Editor-in-Chief for Simply Psychology

BSc (Hons) Psychology, MRes, PhD, University of Manchester

Saul McLeod, PhD., is a qualified psychology teacher with over 18 years of experience in further and higher education. He has been published in peer-reviewed journals, including the Journal of Clinical Psychology.

Learn about our [Editorial Process](#)

[Olivia Guy-Evans, MSc](https://www.simplypsychology.org/author/olivia-guy-evans)

Associate Editor for Simply Psychology

BSc (Hons) Psychology, MSc Psychology of Education

Olivia Guy-Evans is a writer and associate editor for Simply Psychology. She has previously worked in healthcare and educational sectors.

Learn about our [Editorial Process](#)

Learn about our [Editorial Process](#)

Bracketing, also known as epoché or phenomenological reduction, is a technique where researchers set aside (or “bracket out”) their own preconceptions, assumptions, and prior knowledge about a phenomenon to study it more objectively.

Bracketing is a key element of quality in [qualitative research](https://www.simplypsychology.org/qualitative-research-characteristics-design-methods-examples.html), allowing researchers to embrace subjectivity while also managing its potential pitfalls.

This concept was introduced by Edmund Husserl, the founder of [phenomenology](https://www.simplypsychology.org/phenomenology-in-qualitative-research.html), and while its use has expanded beyond that philosophical tradition, its meaning remains somewhat ambiguous.

Scholars have offered various definitions, including:

### What is bracketing?

This lack of a singular definition reflects the complexity of bracketing and the flexibility inherent in qualitative research.

However, this ambiguity also presents challenges, as it can make it difficult to determine how, when, and if to use bracketing effectively.

## Tensions in Bracketing

There is no consensus among scholars on several key aspects of bracketing, creating tensions that researchers must navigate:

The choice of method often depends on the nature of the research and the anticipated emotions or cognitions the researcher might encounter.

## What should be bracketed?

In phenomenological research, bracketing involves suspending preconceptions and judgments to approach the phenomenon with an open mind.

This allows researchers to focus on the participant’s experience without imposing their own interpretations.

Here are some elements that researchers typically bracket during phenomenological research:

## When should bracketing occur?

Bracketing is not a one-time event but an ongoing process that should be integrated into every stage of the research.

This framework emphasizes the **iterative nature** of bracketing, recognizing that preconceptions can arise and need to be addressed throughout the study.

### Bracketing Throughout the Research Process

### Additional Considerations for Timing

Remember that bracketing is not about eliminating preconceptions altogether, as that is often impossible.

It is about **consciously acknowledging, analyzing, and managing** them throughout the research process.

By consistently engaging in bracketing, researchers can enhance the [rigor](https://www.simplypsychology.org/rigor-of-qualitative-research.html), [trustworthiness](https://www.simplypsychology.org/trustworthiness-of-qualitative-data.html), and depth of their qualitative research.

## How should bracketing be conducted?

#### Choosing the right bracketing methods often depends on:

It is crucial to emphasize that bracketing is not about eliminating preconceptions entirely, as that’s often impossible.

The goal is to systematically identify, acknowledge, and manage them to ensure a rigorous and credible research process.

By employing a combination of these bracketing methods and engaging in [continuous reflexivity](https://www.simplypsychology.org/reflexivity-in-qualitative-research.html), researchers can enhance the trustworthiness and depth of their qualitative findings.

## Bracketing in Transcription

Bracketing in transcription is a crucial, yet often overlooked aspect of qualitative research.

It involves the researcher consciously acknowledging their own biases and assumptions while transforming spoken data into a written format.

This process recognizes that transcription is not merely a technical act but an interpretive one, shaped by the researcher’s background, experiences, and research goals.

Here are some key considerations for bracketing in transcription:

## Benefits of Bracketing

Bracketing offers several benefits to qualitative research:

--- SOURCE 5: Epoché in Phenomenology: Husserl's Method of Suspension ---
URL: https://philosophy.institute/western-philosophy/husserl-epoche-phenomenology-method/

SUMMARY:
Epoché is a crucial step in Husserl's phenomenological method, allowing us to suspend our natural assumptions and focus on the pure experience of phenomena.

FULL CONTENT:
![Philosophy Institute](https://philosophy.institute/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Philosophy-Institute-Logo.jpeg)

# Epoché in Phenomenology: Husserl’s Method of Suspension

In the realm of phenomenology, one of the most foundational concepts introduced by Edmund Husserl is the notion of epoché, or “bracketing.” This method holds a crucial place in Husserl’s philosophical project, as it allows us to suspend our natural assumptions and engage with the world as it is experienced, without any preconceived notions. But what exactly does it mean to “bracket” our experience, and why is this process so important for understanding consciousness and the nature of reality? In this post, we’ll dive into Husserl’s epoché, exploring its meaning, implications, and how it forms the cornerstone of phenomenological method.

#### Table of Contents

## What is epoché? 🔗

At its core, the epoché (from the Greek word meaning “suspension”) refers to the act of suspending or “bracketing” all judgments and assumptions about the world. This suspension is not about doubting the existence of things, but rather about setting aside all preconceptions, biases, and taken-for-granted beliefs. In other words, it is the process of withholding judgment about the nature of the external world to focus on pure experience itself.

For Husserl, the purpose of epoché is to return to a more original, direct experience of phenomena, where the subjective and the objective are not yet split. By “bracketing” or suspending the natural attitude—the everyday way we engage with the world based on assumptions—we can get to the essence of our experiences, as they appear to consciousness, unmediated by theoretical frameworks or prejudices.

## Natural attitude vs. transcendental attitude 🔗

To better understand the role of epoché, it’s important to consider Husserl’s distinction between two kinds of attitudes toward the world: the natural attitude and the transcendental attitude.

### The natural attitude 🔗

The natural attitude is the everyday, practical mode of being in the world. It’s how we typically experience reality without questioning or reflecting on the structures behind it. When we engage with the world in the natural attitude, we take for granted that objects exist independently of our perception of them. We interact with the world based on common sense, practical concerns, and social conventions. In this attitude, we do not question the reality of the world around us.

### The transcendental attitude 🔗

In contrast, the transcendental attitude is one of radical reflection. Here, we suspend our everyday assumptions and direct our attention to the structures of consciousness itself. The goal of adopting a transcendental attitude is to gain access to the “transcendental ego”—the pure, pre-reflective consciousness that constitutes the world of experience. This is not about doubting the world, but about getting to the core of how we experience it, free from biases and assumptions.

## How does epoché work? The process of bracketing 🔗

So how exactly does one perform epoché? Husserl provides us with a detailed process, but let’s break it down step by step.

### Suspending beliefs about the external world 🔗

First, the epoché requires us to suspend or “bracket” all beliefs about the existence of the external world. This doesn’t mean denying that objects exist, but rather putting aside the belief in their independent existence. Instead, we focus purely on the way objects appear to consciousness. In this way, we shift from the everyday, unexamined attitude (the natural attitude) to a more rigorous, reflective mode of thought.

For instance, if you’re looking at a chair, instead of thinking, “That’s a chair in the corner of the room,” you would focus on the experience of “chairness”—how the chair appears to your consciousness. You suspend your judgments about the chair’s materiality, purpose, or external properties, and instead, you engage with the pure experience of the chair itself.

### Focusing on the “phenomenon” itself 🔗

Once we have suspended all external assumptions, we begin focusing on the phenomenon itself—the “what” of the experience. This means we examine the way things are given to us in consciousness, as they appear, not as we think them to be. In the case of the chair, you would consider how it appears in your field of perception: its shape, color, and the way it seems to stand in relation to you and the space around it.

### Revealing the intentionality of consciousness 🔗

For Husserl, consciousness is always directed toward something; it is intentional. This means that our consciousness is always about something—whether it’s a chair, an idea, or a memory. Through the epoché, we reveal the intentional nature of our experience. By bracketing all assumptions, we focus on the way consciousness “intends” objects in the world. We explore how our mind actively structures experience, bringing objects into our awareness and making them meaningful.

## The role of epoché in accessing transcendental consciousness 🔗

The ultimate goal of epoché is to allow us to access what Husserl calls “transcendental consciousness” or “pure consciousness.” This is the level of consciousness that is not tied to any particular object or experience but is instead the very ground from which all experience arises. By bracketing everything we assume about the world, we can focus on the structure of consciousness itself—the conditions that make perception and experience possible.

Once we have performed the epoché, we are no longer tied to the particularities of the world. Instead, we can see the world as it is constituted by our consciousness. For Husserl, this is a profound insight, as it reveals that the world is not something external and independent but is, in some sense, dependent on consciousness for its very existence. The epoché uncovers the transcendental nature of experience, which has profound implications for how we understand reality and our place within it.

## Why is epoché important for phenomenology? The foundational role of bracketing 🔗

Epoché is a crucial step for phenomenology because it provides the methodological basis for Husserl’s entire philosophical project. By bracketing our natural attitudes and assumptions, we open the door to a more rigorous exploration of consciousness. Phenomenology is concerned with describing the structures of experience as they are given, without interference from outside theories or metaphysical assumptions. The epoché makes this possible by isolating the essential structures of consciousness that underlie all experience.

Moreover, the epoché allows us to explore the relationship between subject and object, highlighting that objects do not exist independently of our experience of them. This challenges the traditional view of an objective, mind-independent world, and instead emphasizes the role of consciousness in constituting the world as we know it. For phenomenologists, this insight is transformative, opening up new avenues for understanding perception, self-awareness, and even intersubjectivity.

## Epoché and the subjective turn in philosophy 🔗

Husserl’s epoché also marks a turning point in philosophy—a shift toward subjectivity and the first-person perspective. Prior to phenomenology, much of philosophy was concerned with objective truths about the world. Philosophers like Descartes and Kant grappled with questions of existence, knowledge, and reality from the perspective of a detached observer. Husserl, however, took a radically different approach by focusing on how the world is experienced from the standpoint of the individual subject.

The epoché, then, is not just a methodological tool—it is part of a broader movement in philosophy toward understanding the world from the inside out. It encourages a deeper investigation into how we experience reality, rather than assuming what reality is independent of us. This subjective turn in philosophy has had a lasting impact on existentialism, hermeneutics, and contemporary philosophy of mind, among other fields.

## Applications and challenges of epoché 🔗

While the epoché offers profound insights into the nature of consciousness, it also presents significant challenges. For one, the very act of bracketing one’s natural assumptions is difficult. It requires an intense discipline of thought and a willingness to set aside long-held beliefs about the world. Some critics argue that it is impossible to fully bracket all assumptions, as they are often so ingrained in our thinking that they remain implicit even when we try to suspend them.

Furthermore, epoché has practical implications for other areas of philosophy and even psychology. For instance, it challenges traditional metaphysical assumptions about the nature of the world and opens up new possibilities for understanding perception, intentionality, and selfhood. By focusing on pure experience, phenomenologists can explore areas like ethics, aesthetics, and even the experience of time itself, all from the perspective of how these phenomena are given to consciousness.

## Conclusion 🔗

In summary, epoché is a crucial step in Husserl’s phenomenological method, allowing us to suspend our natural assumptions and focus on the pure experience of phenomena. By bracketing the world, we turn our attention to the intentional nature of consciousness, unveiling the transcendental structures that constitute all experience. This method not only shapes phenomenology as a discipline but also marks a philosophical shift toward subjectivity and the first-person perspective.

**What do you think?** How might the concept of epoché change the way we engage with our everyday experiences? Could adopting the transcendental attitude provide a deeper understanding of our own consciousness?

enriched_points.expanded_content: Bracketing, or epoché, is the process of setting aside preconceptions and biases to engage with experiences purely, allowing researchers to focus on the essence of phenomena.
This approach emphasizes understanding individual experiences as they are perceived, revealing insights into consciousness without the interference of external interpretations or judgments.
By suspending beliefs about the external world, researchers can analyze phenomena in their purest form, uncovering the structures of consciousness and how they shape perceptions.
